perturbation.original_text: Any Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement shall be retained in confidence by the Receiving Party and used only for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Any Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement should, to a reasonable extent, be retained in confidence by the Receiving Party and used generally for the Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be retained' to 'should, to a reasonable extent, be retained' weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. 'Used only for the Purpose' becomes 'used generally for the Purpose', which allows for uses beyond the defined purpose, introducing ambiguity. This makes the clause difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: K.S.A. 60-3321 allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the confidentiality obligation with 'should to a reasonable extent' and 'generally,' the modified clause provides a loophole, making it more difficult to obtain an injunction under KUTSA because the Receiving Party can argue they made a 'reasonable' effort, even if the information was misappropriated.

Misappropriation of trade secret; injunctive or other protective relief. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: L. 1981, ch. 214, § 2; L. 1988, ch. 221, § 1; July 1. perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the confidentiality obligation, creating a contradiction. The original text states 'shall be retained in confidence,' a clear mandate. The changed text uses 'should, to a reasonable extent, be retained,' introducing ambiguity and discretion. This directly undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement. The Law Explanation highlights that K.S.A. 60-3321 allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. The change makes it more difficult to obtain an injunction because the Receiving Party can argue they made a 'reasonable' effort. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 both confirm that K.S.A. 60-3321 allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation. The added ambiguity weakens the protection intended by this statute, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall use the same degree of care as it uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but no less than reasonable care, to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor to use a degree of care it finds appropriate to protect Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall use the same degree of care as it uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but no less than reasonable care' with 'shall endeavor to use a degree of care it finds appropriate' provides significantly more discretion to the Receiving Party. The original text sets a clear standard of care, the modified text makes it subjective and difficult to determine if the required care has been taken.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text outlines a reasonable standard of care, whereas the changed text introduces a subjective and potentially lower standard ('a degree of care it finds appropriate'). If a trade secret is misappropriated due to negligence permitted by the modified clause, it weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to seek injunctive relief under K.S.A. 60-3321.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a standard of care equivalent to protecting one's own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care. The modified contract introduces a subjective standard, allowing the Receiving Party to use 'a degree of care it finds appropriate,' potentially undermining the protection of trade secrets. K.S.A. 60-3321(a) allows for injunctive relief against misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the standard of care, the modified clause makes it harder for the Disclosing Party to prove misappropriation due to negligence, as the 'appropriate' level of care is now subject to the Receiving Party's interpretation. This directly contradicts the intent of K.S.A. 60-3321, making injunctive relief less accessible.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the completion or termination of any discussions between the parties, or at any time within fourteen (14) days of receipt of a written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall (i) promptly return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information disclosed in tangible form and copies thereof; or (ii) promptly destroy such Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) and certify their destruction to the Disclosing Party .
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the completion or termination of any discussions between the parties, the Receiving Party should consider returning or destroying the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the mandatory 'shall promptly return' or 'shall promptly destroy' with the discretionary 'should consider returning or destroying' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information after the discussions are complete. The original text provided specific actions to be taken, while the revised text introduces ambiguity regarding whether any action will be taken.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text makes it unclear if the Receiving Party is obligated to return or destroy confidential information, thus weakening protection. This ambiguous obligation makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to obtain an injunction under K.S.A. 60-3321 if the Receiving Party continues to use the trade secret after discussions end, as they can claim they were only considering returning or destroying it.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates specific actions ('shall promptly return' or 'shall promptly destroy') regarding confidential information. The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'should consider returning or destroying,' introducing ambiguity about whether any action will be taken. The Law Explanation suggests this weakened obligation could hinder the Disclosing Party from obtaining an injunction under K.S.A. 60-3321. The scraped law snippet from Kansas Statutes § 60-3321 (2023) discusses injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the obligation ambiguous, the modification undermines the enforceability of protecting trade secrets, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to comply with all applicable export control legislation with respect to Confidenti al Information received hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party should make reasonable efforts to comply with applicable export control legislation with respect to Confidential Information received hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to comply' to 'should make reasonable efforts to comply' transforms a strict obligation into a discretionary one. The term 'reasonable efforts' is vague and subjective, leaving room for interpretation and potential non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR mandates strict compliance with export control laws. By changing the language to 'should make reasonable efforts,' the modified clause contradicts the mandatory nature of EAR requirements. If confidential information subject to export controls is mishandled due to a lack of strict compliance, it would be a violation of 15 CFR § 732.3.

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires strict compliance with export control legislation, whereas the changed text weakens this obligation by stating that each party 'should make reasonable efforts to comply.' This introduces ambiguity and discretion where the law, as suggested by the Law Explanation referencing 15 CFR § 732.3, mandates strict compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774, reinforcing that export control is governed by specific and regulated requirements, not simply 'reasonable efforts.' The change directly undermines the obligatory nature of export control laws, creating a contradiction.
